title: Inequalities
learning_objectives: Solve linear inequalities using inverse operations.
Reverse the inequality sign when multiplying or dividing by a negative.
Count integer solutions within a range.

pedagogical_model.sequence: I do → we do → you do

opening.builds_on: Solving linear equations by inverse operations.

items.prompt: If x > 3, is x = 3 a solution?
Solve −x < 4. Why does it become x > −4?
items.answer: Multiplying/dividing by a negative reverses the inequality.

retrieval_starter.purpose: Retrieves equation-style solving and surfaces the single rule that makes inequalities different: flip when you multiply or divide by a negative.
retrieval_starter.if_students_struggle: If the flip is unintuitive, test x = −10 in −x < 4: −(−10) = 10, which is NOT < 4, confirming the reversed solution.

opening.prior_knowledge_bridge: Inequalities solve exactly like equations — with one twist from Q5: multiplying or dividing both sides by a negative reverses the direction. Master that and you also handle compound inequalities (a range) and 'how many integers' questions the GMAT likes.
opening.learning_intention: By the end you can solve linear and compound inequalities, flipping the sign correctly, and count integer solutions in a range.
success_criteria: I solve inequalities with inverse operations like equations.
I reverse the sign exactly when I divide/multiply by a negative.
I can read a compound inequality as a range and count its integers.

opening_script.move: Check; test a value to justify the flip in Q5.
Bridge: same as equations + flip rule; preview compound ranges.

i_do.explanation: Subtract 2: −3x ≤ 9. Divide by −3 and FLIP the sign: x ≥ −3.
think_aloud_steps: Divide by −3 — because it's negative, reverse ≤ to ≥: x ≥ −3.
i_do.key_takeaway: Solve like an equation, but reverse the inequality whenever you multiply or divide by a negative number.

immediate_feedback.if_correct: Right — x > 3 (no flip, since 4 is positive).
immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: You only flip for a negative divisor; here it's +4.

we_do.explanation: Subtract 5: −2x ≥ −4. Divide by −2 and flip: x ≤ 2.

immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: The −2 divisor forces the flip; ≥ becomes ≤.

you_do.stem: If −2 < x ≤ 3 and x is an integer, how many values can x take?
you_do.explanation: Integers with −2 < x ≤ 3 are −1, 0, 1, 2, 3 — five values (−2 excluded, 3 included).

you_do.stem: If 3 − 2x > 7, which of the following must be true?

sample_held_out_items.explanation: Multiply both sides by −2 and flip: x < −6.

mastery_check.definition: A topic is mastered at >=85% correct on new/held-out-style questions across >=2 spaced sessions with delay (PRD Section 9.2). Flashcard recall alone is not mastery; application under mixed, delayed conditions is the bar.
conditions: >=85% correct on new/held-out-style items (not flashcard recall) per session
sustained across >=2 spaced sessions with a delay between them
per-topic memory surfaced only after >=20 graded reviews (PRD give-up rule)
per-topic performance surfaced only after >=8 application attempts (PRD give-up rule)
mastery_check.on_fail: Not yet: route every miss to the required error log (tag why: content gap / reasoning flaw / timing / careless / trap), schedule spaced re-practice via topic-aware scheduling, then re-enter the we-do -> you-do loop before re-testing.

citations.name: Khan Academy — Inequalities
citations.note: One- and two-step inequalities and the sign-flip rule.

citations.name: GMAC — GMAT Focus Edition (official exam overview)
